passage: Economists studying urban development have observed that cities investing heavily in public transit infrastructure tend to experience greater growth in median household income. A recent analysis of five metropolitan areas between 2015 and 2023 suggests that transit investment of at least $500 million is associated with income growth exceeding 20%.
stem: Which choice most effectively uses data from the figure to support the economists' claim?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Metro areas that invested over $500 million in transit -- Portland ($620M, 24% income growth) and Denver ($780M, 27% income growth) -- both saw income growth above 20%, while areas investing less than $500 million saw growth below 15%.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Denver invested $780 million in public transit and experienced 27% median income growth, the highest of any metro area in the study.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nashville invested $310 million in transit and experienced 14% income growth, while Tampa invested $180 million and saw only 8% growth.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Portland's transit investment of $620 million was lower than Denver's $780 million, yet Portland's 24% income growth was only 3 percentage points below Denver's 27%.

explanation: The claim links transit investment of $500M+ to income growth over 20%. Choice A shows both high-investment cities (Portland $620M/24% and Denver $780M/27%) exceeded the 20% threshold, while lower-investment areas stayed below 15%, establishing the pattern. Choice B focuses on only one city. Choice C discusses low-investment cities without connecting them to the high-investment threshold. Choice D compares two high-investment cities to each other rather than demonstrating the association between investment level and income growth.

distractorRationale.A: Correct: cites both high-investment cities exceeding 20% growth and notes low-investment cities fell short, establishing the claimed association.
distractorRationale.B: Wrong (incomplete evidence): mentions only Denver without showing the broader pattern across investment levels needed to support a general association.
distractorRationale.C: Wrong (irrelevant data): cites two below-threshold cities that don't directly address whether the $500M+ threshold is linked to 20%+ growth.
distractorRationale.D: Wrong (wrong comparison): compares two high-investment cities to each other rather than comparing high-investment to low-investment outcomes.

imageDescription: Data table titled 'Transit Investment and Median Household Income Growth, 2015-2023.' Columns: Metro Area, Transit Investment ($ millions), Income Growth (%). Rows: Denver - $780M - 27%; Portland - $620M - 24%; Nashville - $310M - 14%; Tampa - $180M - 8%; Tucson - $95M - 6%. Source: Urban Economics Research Group, 2024.

chartData.title: Transit Investment and Median Household Income Growth, 2015-2023
